text.title: Z-test and Sample Size Formulas
text.summary: Two-proportion z-test formula and sample size calculation with a worked numerical example.

rule.statement: Use a two-proportion z-test for conversion rate comparisons; always two-tailed unless a regression would never be shipped.
rule.rationale: Two-tailed tests do not assume the direction of the effect, preventing directional bias that inflates Type I error for the expected direction.

example.code:
Inputs: n1 = 1000, x1 = 50 (A: 5.0%) n2 = 1000, x2 = 62 (B: 6.2%)
p_pooled = (50 + 62) / (1000 + 1000) = 0.056 SE = sqrt(0.056 * 0.944 * (1/1000 + 1/1000)) = 0.0103 z = (0.062 - 0.050) / 0.0103 = 1.17 p-value (two-tailed) ≈ 0.24
Result: NOT significant (p > 0.05) More data needed — difference may be real but unproven.

section.title: Sample Size Formula

rule.statement: Calculate n per variant as ceil(16 * p * (1-p) / MDE^2) at 95% confidence / 80% power; use the exact statsmodels formula for asymmetric splits.
rule.rationale: The simplified formula (16 * p(1-p)/MDE^2) is accurate for 50/50 splits. Asymmetric traffic splits require the full formula via NormalIndPower().solve_power().

example.code:
Full formula: n = 2 * [(Z_alpha/2 + Z_beta)^2 * p * (1-p)] / MDE^2 Z_alpha/2 = 1.96 (95% confidence) Z_beta = 0.84 (80% power)
Simplified: n per variant ≈ 16 * p * (1-p) / MDE^2
Example: baseline=5%, MDE=10% relative (0.5% absolute) n = 16 * 0.05 * 0.95 / 0.005^2 = 30,400 per variant

section.reference: Pre-computed n values by baseline and MDE for quick planning

section.title: Sample Size by Baseline and MDE
section.p: Higher baseline rates and larger MDE targets both reduce required sample size. A 5% relative MDE at 1% baseline requires ~314,000 per variant; the same relative MDE at 50% baseline requires only ~3,200.
